heroOverview: Hemoperitoneum is the accumulation of blood inside the peritoneal cavity — the space surrounding the abdominal organs — and it is always a sign of significant internal bleeding from a solid organ, vessel, or viscus. Traumatic injury to the liver or spleen is the single most common cause in high-income countries, accounting for roughly 40-50% of cases, but non-traumatic rupture from ectopic pregnancy, ruptured aortic aneurysm, or spontaneous tumour haemorrhage is responsible for a growing share of emergency presentations. The volume of blood can range from a clinically silent few millilitres detectable only on imaging to several litres causing haemorrhagic shock. Even moderate hemoperitoneum (500 mL or more) can produce haemodynamic instability within minutes. Prompt diagnosis — most efficiently by bedside FAST ultrasound in the trauma bay — and rapid source control, whether by surgery or interventional radiology, are the cornerstones of management.

keyStats.prevalence: Traumatic hemoperitoneum occurs in approximately 20-40% of patients with significant abdominal trauma; precise population incidence is not routinely tracked but haemoperitoneum from all causes is estimated at 6-10 per 100,000 emergency admissions annually
keyStats.demographics: Traumatic cases peak in males aged 15-45 (consistent with injury demographics); ectopic pregnancy-related hemoperitoneum is the leading cause in women of reproductive age
keyStats.avgAge: Varies markedly by aetiology: traumatic cases skew to young adults; spontaneous bleeding from tumour or aneurysm is more common after age 60
keyStats.globalCases: Road-traffic injuries alone cause an estimated 4.4 million deaths annually worldwide, with haemoperitoneum a principal mechanism of preventable death

definition: Hemoperitoneum (ICD-10: K66.1) is the abnormal presence of free blood within the peritoneal cavity, defined by imaging or at surgery as fluid with Hounsfield units consistent with blood on CT, or blood-tinged return on diagnostic peritoneal lavage. The peritoneal cavity normally contains only a small volume of serous fluid; blood is highly irritating to the peritoneum, producing pain, tenderness, and — over hours — a chemical peritonitis. Hemoperitoneum is classified as traumatic (blunt or penetrating abdominal injury) or non-traumatic (also called spontaneous), the latter encompassing ectopic pregnancy, ruptured hepatocellular carcinoma, splenic haemorrhage from portal hypertension, anticoagulant-related bleeding, and ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm. Volume and rate of bleeding determine haemodynamic stability and urgency of intervention. In haemodynamically unstable patients, immediate operative or endovascular control is required; stable patients with contained bleeding may be managed non-operatively under close observation. The condition falls under the gastroenterology and general or hepatobiliary surgery specialties, with interventional radiology increasingly central to haemostasis.

typesClassification.description: Caused by blunt (motor vehicle collision, fall) or penetrating (stab wound, gunshot) abdominal injury, most often injuring the spleen, liver, or mesenteric vessels.
The leading non-traumatic cause in women of childbearing age; a tubal pregnancy ruptures into the peritoneal cavity, producing sudden haemorrhage and haemodynamic collapse.
Hepatocellular carcinoma, hepatic adenoma, and renal angiomyolipoma can rupture spontaneously, causing potentially massive haemoperitoneum — particularly in patients with underlying cirrhosis.
Rupture of an aortic aneurysm into the peritoneum carries mortality above 80% without surgical repair; survival depends on immediate intervention.
Patients on warfarin, direct oral anticoagulants, or thrombolytics can bleed spontaneously from mesenteric or visceral sites, increasingly recognised with wider anticoagulant use.
Bleeding after laparoscopy, liver biopsy, percutaneous nephrostomy, or other abdominal procedures; usually detected within 12-24 hours.

primarySymptoms: Sudden-onset, diffuse or localised abdominal pain that may be severe and constant, reflecting peritoneal irritation by blood.
Abdominal rigidity or guarding — involuntary tensing of the abdominal muscles — indicating peritoneal inflammation.
Hypotension (systolic blood pressure below 90 mmHg) and a rapid, weak pulse from haemorrhagic volume loss.
Pallor, cold clammy skin, and diaphoresis as the body diverts blood away from the periphery to maintain core perfusion.
Dizziness, light-headedness, or frank syncope when blood loss reduces cerebral perfusion.
Shoulder-tip or scapular pain (Kehr's sign), referred from diaphragmatic irritation by sub-phrenic blood.
Abdominal distension that may develop progressively as blood accumulates in the peritoneum.
Nausea and vomiting triggered by peritoneal irritation and haemodynamic stress.
Rapid, shallow breathing as pain limits diaphragm movement and falling haemoglobin reduces oxygen delivery.
In ectopic pregnancy rupture: severe pelvic pain with missed or irregular period and a positive pregnancy test.
earlyWarningSigns: Progressive lower abdominal or pelvic pain in a woman with a late or missed period — ectopic pregnancy must be excluded.
Increasing abdominal pain and tenderness after abdominal trauma, even without obvious external injury.
Unexplained tachycardia (heart rate above 100 beats per minute) in a patient with known liver tumour or cirrhosis.
A sudden worsening of abdominal pain in a patient on anticoagulant therapy.
emergencySigns: Haemodynamic instability — hypotension, rapid pulse, confusion — after abdominal trauma or sudden abdominal pain: call emergency services immediately.
Severe pelvic pain with a positive pregnancy test and hypotension — likely ruptured ectopic pregnancy, requiring emergency surgery.
A pulsating abdominal mass with sudden back or abdominal pain in a person over 60 — ruptured aortic aneurysm.
Abdominal rigidity with rapid deterioration in any patient — indicates surgical emergency.
Collapse or loss of consciousness accompanied by abdominal tenderness — emergent investigation and resuscitation required.

causes.cause: Blunt abdominal trauma (splenic or hepatic laceration)
causes.description: High-energy deceleration injuries tear the solid organ parenchyma or its capsule; the spleen is the most commonly injured abdominal organ in blunt trauma, followed by the liver, with bleeding ranging from a self-limiting capsular tear to exsanguinating vascular avulsion.

An embryo implanted in the fallopian tube distends and ultimately ruptures the tube, releasing blood and gestational tissue into the peritoneum; the quantity of bleeding can be rapidly life-threatening and this is a leading cause of maternal death in the first trimester.

An aneurysm in which the maximum diameter exceeds roughly 5.5 cm has a substantially elevated rupture risk; when the posterior or anterior wall gives way, blood streams into the retroperitoneum or peritoneum under systemic pressure, and survival demands operative repair within minutes to hours.

Hepatocellular carcinoma and large hepatic adenomas (especially in women taking oral contraceptives) can rupture spontaneously, releasing blood into the peritoneum; this complicates approximately 10-15% of HCC presentations and carries high acute mortality.

Percutaneous liver biopsy, laparoscopic surgery, ERCP with sphincterotomy, or transhepatic procedures may injure intra-abdominal vessels; early postoperative haemoperitoneum within 24 hours requires imaging to detect and often angiographic embolisation or re-operation to control.

Warfarin, heparin, direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs), and systemic thrombolytics lower coagulation capacity; spontaneous mesenteric, omental, or visceral haemorrhage is an increasingly recognised complication, particularly at supratherapeutic drug levels or with concurrent trauma.

Stab wounds and gunshot wounds directly transect abdominal vessels or viscera; the trajectory of injury determines which structures bleed, and haemorrhage control in the operating theatre is the definitive management.

riskFactors.factor: Abdominal trauma (vehicle collisions, falls, sport injury)
riskFactors.description: High-energy blunt or penetrating trauma to the abdomen is the most common precipitating event; wearing a seatbelt and bicycle helmet reduces the risk of solid-organ injury in crashes.

riskFactors.factor: Pregnancy in the fallopian tube (ectopic pregnancy)
riskFactors.description: Risk factors for ectopic implantation include previous pelvic inflammatory disease, tubal surgery, previous ectopic, and assisted reproduction; early ultrasound screening can detect the ectopic before rupture.
Underlying liver tumours, particularly HCC in a cirrhotic liver, are prone to spontaneous haemorrhage; HCC carries roughly a 10% lifetime risk of rupture.

riskFactors.factor: Abdominal aortic aneurysm above 5.5 cm diameter
riskFactors.description: Untreated large aneurysms have a 25-40% annual rupture risk; elective repair at appropriate thresholds and ultrasound screening in high-risk males aged 65+ prevent rupture.
Supratherapeutic anticoagulation, DOAC drug interactions, and thrombolytic agents increase bleeding risk; regular INR monitoring and dose adjustment reduce the risk in patients on warfarin.
Portal hypertension generates venous collaterals and coagulopathy, both of which predispose to spontaneous intraperitoneal bleeding from ruptured varices or tumour.

riskFactors.factor: Recent abdominal surgery or percutaneous procedure
riskFactors.description: Post-procedural haemorrhage risk is highest in the first 24-48 hours; peri-procedural correction of coagulopathy and haemostatic technique reduce incidence.

affectedDemographics: Young males aged 15-45 in whom trauma is the predominant cause
Women of reproductive age in whom ruptured ectopic pregnancy is a leading cause
Patients with hepatocellular carcinoma, particularly on a background of cirrhosis
Elderly adults (over 60) with undiagnosed or known abdominal aortic aneurysm
Patients on therapeutic anticoagulation or who have received thrombolytic therapy
Individuals with portal hypertension from any cause
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of hemoperitoneum prioritises speed in the unstable patient and accuracy in the stable one. In the trauma setting, the Focused Assessment with Sonography in Trauma (FAST) is performed in the resuscitation bay, taking under two minutes to detect free intraperitoneal fluid in the Morison's pouch, splenorenal space, and pelvis; a positive FAST in a haemodynamically unstable patient with blunt trauma is an indication for immediate surgery without further imaging. In stable patients, contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis is the reference standard, precisely localising bleeding and characterising injured organs using a grading system (e.g., AAST solid organ injury scale). Blood tests reveal a falling haematocrit or haemoglobin, coagulopathy, and elevated lactate indicating tissue hypoperfusion. In suspected non-traumatic haemoperitoneum, a urine or serum beta-HCG must be checked immediately in all women of childbearing age to exclude ectopic pregnancy. Diagnostic peritoneal aspiration (DPA) — inserting a needle into the peritoneum and aspirating blood — confirms haemoperitoneum rapidly when imaging is unavailable; a return of non-clotting blood is diagnostic. In women with suspected ruptured ectopic, transvaginal ultrasound combined with a positive HCG provides the diagnosis.

diagnosticTests.test: FAST (Focused Assessment with Sonography in Trauma)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Bedside ultrasound to detect free intraperitoneal fluid within minutes; the primary tool in the trauma resuscitation bay for unstable patients.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A handheld ultrasound probe is applied to four abdominal and pelvic windows while you lie flat; the whole scan takes 2-5 minutes and involves no radiation or needles.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Definitive anatomical imaging to identify bleeding source, quantify injury, and plan management in haemodynamically stable patients.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: An intravenous iodine dye is injected and you pass through the CT scanner; the scan itself takes 5-10 minutes and requires checking kidney function beforehand.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Mandatory in all women of reproductive age presenting with abdominal pain and haemoperitoneum to rule in or out ectopic pregnancy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood draw or rapid urine dip; serum levels can return within 30 minutes in an emergency laboratory.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood count, coagulation screen, and lactate
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies haemorrhage (falling haemoglobin), detects coagulopathy, and measures lactate as a marker of haemorrhagic shock and tissue hypoperfusion.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard arm blood draw; results return within 30-60 minutes in most emergency settings.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Direct sampling of peritoneal fluid by needle insertion when ultrasound is unavailable or equivocal; immediate confirmation of blood in the abdomen.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Local anaesthetic is injected to the lower abdomen and a needle advanced into the peritoneum; the presence of blood or bile-stained fluid is immediately diagnostic.

diagnosticTests.purpose: In women with suspected ectopic pregnancy, provides higher resolution pelvic imaging to locate or exclude an ectopic implantation and detect pelvic blood.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small ultrasound probe is gently placed in the vaginal canal; takes approximately 10-15 minutes and is more sensitive than external ultrasound for early pregnancy complications.

treatmentOverview: Treatment is guided by haemodynamic stability and the underlying cause of bleeding. The immediately unstable patient requires simultaneous resuscitation and haemorrhage control. Initial resuscitation follows damage-control principles: permissive hypotension (targeting systolic blood pressure of 80-90 mmHg until surgical control), early transfusion of packed red cells and fresh frozen plasma in a balanced ratio (1:1 or 1:1:1 with platelets), intravenous tranexamic acid 1 g within 3 hours of injury to inhibit fibrinolysis, and aggressive correction of hypothermia and coagulopathy. Haemostasis strategy is then tailored to the source. Traumatic solid-organ injury is managed non-operatively in approximately 60-80% of haemodynamically stable patients with hepatic injury and 50-70% of those with splenic injury; angioembolisation of arterial bleeding sources is central to this non-operative approach and avoids the morbidity of laparotomy. Unstable patients require emergency laparotomy with damage-control surgery: pack and temporise bleeding, close temporarily, and return for definitive repair 24-48 hours later once physiology is corrected. Ruptured ectopic pregnancy requires emergency salpingectomy or salpingostomy. Ruptured AAA requires immediate open or endovascular aortic repair. Spontaneous hepatic tumour haemorrhage is managed with angioembolisation as first line, followed by resection once the patient is stable and liver function permits. Post-procedural and anticoagulant-related haemoperitoneum often resolves with reversal of anticoagulation and supportive measures, reserving embolisation for confirmed arterial bleeding.

medicalTreatments.name: Damage-control resuscitation (blood products and tranexamic acid)
medicalTreatments.description: Early administration of red cells, fresh frozen plasma, and platelets in balanced ratio, combined with tranexamic acid 1 g IV within 3 hours of injury, reduces coagulopathic bleeding. This pharmacological strategy has been shown to improve survival in haemorrhagic shock.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: The CRASH-2 trial showed tranexamic acid given within 3 hours of injury reduced all-cause mortality by approximately 9% relative to placebo in traumatic haemorrhage.

medicalTreatments.name: Anticoagulant reversal agents
medicalTreatments.description: Vitamin K and prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC) reverse warfarin; idarucizumab reverses dabigatran; andexanet alfa reverses factor Xa inhibitors. Rapid reversal is critical when anticoagulation is the precipitating factor.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: PCC achieves INR normalisation (below 1.5) within 15-30 minutes in over 90% of warfarin-associated haemoperitoneum cases.

medicalTreatments.name: Vasopressors (noradrenaline)
medicalTreatments.description: Used in haemorrhagic shock refractory to fluid and blood products, as a bridge to definitive surgical control; they maintain perfusion pressure at the cost of peripheral vasoconstriction.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Maintains mean arterial pressure above 65 mmHg in 70-80% of patients as a bridge while surgical haemostasis is prepared.

medicalTreatments.name: Methotrexate for unruptured ectopic pregnancy
medicalTreatments.description: Appropriate only when the ectopic pregnancy has not ruptured, is small (sac below 3.5 cm), and the patient is haemodynamically stable; it causes trophoblast lysis without surgery. Once haemoperitoneum is confirmed, surgery is required.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves tubal resolution in approximately 70-90% of carefully selected unruptured ectopic pregnancies; not applicable once rupture and haemoperitoneum have occurred.

surgicalOptions.name: Emergency laparotomy with damage-control surgery
surgicalOptions.description: An immediate open abdominal operation to identify and control bleeding sources by packing, clamping, or ligation; definitive repair is deferred to a second operation 24-48 hours later once coagulopathy and hypothermia are corrected.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Survival to discharge in haemodynamically unstable traumatic haemoperitoneum reaches approximately 70-85% in major trauma centres with damage-control protocols.

surgicalOptions.name: Angiographic embolisation (interventional radiology)
surgicalOptions.description: A catheter is advanced from the femoral artery to the bleeding vessel under fluoroscopy and the vessel is occluded with coils, gel foam, or particles; the least invasive definitive haemostatic option in stable patients.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Primary technical success in controlling arterial haemoperitoneum is 85-95%; re-bleeding requiring repeat embolisation or surgery occurs in 5-15% of cases.

surgicalOptions.name: Emergency salpingectomy for ruptured ectopic pregnancy
surgicalOptions.description: The affected fallopian tube is removed laparoscopically or via laparotomy to stop the haemorrhage and prevent recurrence in the same tube; the contralateral tube is preserved.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Achieves haemorrhage control in virtually 100% of cases; subsequent intrauterine pregnancy rates are approximately 55-65% in women with one remaining normal tube.

surgicalOptions.name: Open or endovascular repair of ruptured AAA
surgicalOptions.description: The rupture is controlled by aortic cross-clamping (open surgery) or by deploying an endovascular stent-graft across the aneurysm; both approaches halt bleeding but endovascular repair (EVAR) is associated with lower 30-day mortality in selected anatomy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 30-day survival after ruptured AAA repair is approximately 40-50% overall; centres performing high volumes report survival above 60%. Untreated, mortality approaches 100%.

surgicalOptions.name: Laparoscopic or open hepatic resection for tumour rupture
surgicalOptions.description: Once the acute haemorrhage from a ruptured liver tumour has been controlled by embolisation, elective hepatic resection removes the source and, in HCC, may be curative in selected patients.
surgicalOptions.successRate: In Child-Pugh A HCC patients, resection after stabilisation achieves 5-year survival of approximately 25-35%; outcomes in Child-Pugh B/C cirrhosis are substantially worse.

alternativeTreatments.name: Non-operative management (NOM) with close monitoring
alternativeTreatments.description: In haemodynamically stable patients with grade I-III solid organ injuries, NOM under ICU monitoring avoids surgery in 60-80% of cases; requires serial haematocrit checks, bedside imaging, and immediate access to operating theatre if deterioration occurs.

alternativeTreatments.name: Topical haemostatic agents and fibrin sealants intraoperatively
alternativeTreatments.description: Products such as Surgicel, Floseal, and fibrin glue are applied directly to liver or splenic lacerations during damage-control surgery to augment surgical haemostasis and reduce re-bleeding.

whySeeSpecialist: Hemoperitoneum is a surgical emergency requiring immediate assessment by a general or trauma surgeon. Any patient presenting to an emergency department with abdominal trauma, suspected ectopic pregnancy, or sudden severe abdominal pain with hypotension requires immediate specialist evaluation. Interventional radiology is often central to haemostasis. Long-term follow-up by a hepatobiliary surgeon or gastroenterologist is needed if the cause is a liver tumour or portal hypertension.
doctorSelectionGuide: For traumatic haemoperitoneum, seek a level I or II trauma centre with 24-hour trauma surgery and interventional radiology capability. For ruptured ectopic, any emergency gynaecological unit with laparoscopic capability is appropriate. For spontaneous hepatic or AAA-related haemoperitoneum, a hospital with a vascular or hepatobiliary surgery unit and on-site interventional radiology provides the best outcomes.
preventionStrategies: Wear seatbelts, helmets, and appropriate protective equipment to reduce abdominal injury severity in crashes.
Obtain early first-trimester ultrasound to locate a pregnancy and exclude ectopic implantation in women with risk factors (previous PID, tubal surgery, IVF).
Accept elective repair of abdominal aortic aneurysms when they reach or exceed 5.5 cm — ultrasound surveillance programmes in men aged 65+ detect enlarging aneurysms before rupture.
Maintain anticoagulant therapy within therapeutic range with regular INR monitoring; review all interacting medications promptly.
Monitor liver tumours with regular imaging; treat HCC in cirrhotic patients before the lesion reaches a size associated with spontaneous rupture risk.
lifestyleModifications: Avoid contact sports and high-impact activities for 4-8 weeks after non-operative management of a solid-organ injury, as per trauma surgery guidance.
Do not use NSAIDs, aspirin, or anticoagulants without medical supervision if you have underlying liver disease or a known abdominal tumour.
Manage cardiovascular risk factors (hypertension, smoking, hyperlipidaemia) that accelerate aortic aneurysm growth.
Maintain abstinence from alcohol if recovering from hepatic injury to support liver healing.
Attend all scheduled imaging follow-up after non-operative solid-organ injury management to confirm resolution.

recommended: Iron-rich foods (lean meat, legumes, fortified cereals) and folate-rich vegetables during recovery to support haemoglobin regeneration
Adequate protein intake to support tissue repair after surgery
Vitamin C-rich foods to enhance iron absorption
Well-hydrated diet to support renal clearance of absorbed blood breakdown products
avoid: Alcohol during recovery, which impairs platelet function and hepatic regeneration
NSAIDs and aspirin without medical supervision, which increase bleeding risk
Very high-fat diets in the immediate postoperative period, which can increase metabolic demand on a recovering liver

exerciseGuidelines: Return to physical activity is individualised by injury grade and management approach. After non-operative management of grade I-II splenic or hepatic injuries, most patients return to light activity at 4-6 weeks and full activity including sport at 3-6 months, guided by follow-up imaging confirmation of healing. Post-laparotomy patients begin walking within 24-48 hours and progress gradually, avoiding heavy lifting for 6-8 weeks.
dailyManagement: Attend all follow-up imaging appointments — CT or ultrasound — after non-operative management to confirm injury resolution.
Monitor for warning signs of delayed bleeding: worsening abdominal pain, light-headedness, or rapidly increasing bruising.
Take oral iron supplementation if prescribed to replenish stores after significant blood loss.
Keep all anticoagulation clinic appointments and take medications exactly as prescribed.
Contact emergency services immediately if abdominal pain recurs sharply or you develop dizziness or collapse during recovery.
prognosis: Prognosis varies dramatically by cause, speed of diagnosis, and access to surgical care. Traumatic haemoperitoneum in haemodynamically stable patients managed non-operatively has excellent outcomes, with over 90% avoiding surgery and returning to full function. In haemodynamically unstable patients requiring emergency surgery, survival at major trauma centres reaches 70-85% with damage-control protocols. Ruptured ectopic pregnancy, treated promptly, carries very low mortality in high-income settings but remains a leading cause of maternal death in countries with limited surgical access. Ruptured AAA has overall 30-day mortality of approximately 50-60%, underscoring the importance of pre-rupture surveillance and elective repair. Spontaneous HCC rupture carries high short-term mortality (15-30%) but selected patients can proceed to definitive resection. The long-term prognosis is primarily governed by the underlying condition — liver disease, malignancy, or vascular disease — rather than the haemoperitoneum episode itself.
recoveryTimeline: After non-operative management of a moderate solid-organ injury, haematocrit stabilises within 24-48 hours, abdominal pain eases over three to seven days, and most patients are discharged within three to five days. Injury healing on imaging takes four to eight weeks for grade II injuries and up to three to six months for higher-grade lacerations. After laparotomy, hospital stay ranges from five to fourteen days; full recovery takes six to twelve weeks. Ectopic pregnancy recovery is typically two to four weeks after laparoscopic salpingectomy.
complications: Haemorrhagic shock — insufficient circulating blood volume triggers multi-organ failure; the most immediately life-threatening complication, requiring simultaneous resuscitation and haemostasis.
Delayed splenic or hepatic rupture — an initial self-contained injury can re-bleed days to weeks later; occurs in approximately 5-10% of non-operatively managed solid-organ injuries.
Intraperitoneal infection — blood is an excellent culture medium; untreated or incompletely drained haemoperitoneum can become infected, causing peritonitis or abscess.
Abdominal compartment syndrome — massive transfusion, bowel oedema, or packing raises intra-abdominal pressure above 20 mmHg, impairing organ perfusion and requiring decompressive laparotomy.
Coagulopathy of trauma — the triad of hypothermia, acidosis, and dilutional coagulopathy from massive transfusion amplifies ongoing bleeding and is a major determinant of mortality.
Infertility after ectopic pregnancy — salpingectomy removes the affected tube; risk of future ectopic in the remaining tube is approximately 10-15%.

supportResources.name: NIDDK — Ectopic Pregnancy
supportResources.description: National Institutes of Health resource on reproductive and abdominal conditions for patients seeking authoritative guidance.

supportResources.name: NHS — Ectopic pregnancy
supportResources.description: UK NHS plain-English guide covering recognition, emergency management, and recovery from ectopic pregnancy, the leading non-traumatic cause of haemoperitoneum in women.

supportResources.name: American Association for the Surgery of Trauma (AAST) — Patient Resources
supportResources.description: Professional society for trauma surgeons; provides organ injury grading scales used worldwide to guide management of traumatic haemoperitoneum.

sources.title: Stassen NA et al. Selective nonoperative management of blunt splenic injury: an Eastern Association for the Surgery of Trauma practice management guideline. J Trauma. 2012
CRASH-2 Collaborators. Effects of tranexamic acid on death, vascular occlusive events, and blood transfusion in trauma patients. Lancet. 2010
Tran A et al. Diagnosis of intra-abdominal hemorrhage by FAST exam. J Trauma Acute Care Surg. 2021

confusedWithConditions.name: Appendicitis with peritonitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Appendicitis produces peritoneal signs and acute abdomen but from infection rather than haemorrhage; there is no internal bleeding, haemoglobin remains stable, and FAST is negative for blood.

confusedWithConditions.name: Perforated gastric ulcer with haemorrhage
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: A perforated ulcer causes peritonitis from gastric contents and occasionally small amounts of blood, but the dominant pathology is chemical peritonitis from acid, not haemorrhagic shock.

confusedWithConditions.name: Ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Ruptured AAA is itself a cause of hemoperitoneum — but patients presenting with a pulsatile abdominal mass or known large aneurysm should be triaged to emergency vascular surgery, not just general abdominal assessment.

relatedConditions.name: Ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm
relatedConditions.relevance: A ruptured AAA is the most immediately lethal cause of non-traumatic haemoperitoneum; the two diagnoses are inseparable in the vascular emergency context.

relatedConditions.name: Pelvic inflammatory disease
relatedConditions.relevance: PID is a risk factor for ectopic pregnancy, the leading non-traumatic cause of haemoperitoneum in women of reproductive age.

relatedConditions.name: Acute appendicitis with localized peritonitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Frequently considered in the differential of acute abdomen; peritoneal findings overlap but haemorrhage versus infection is the key distinguishing feature.

relatedConditions.name: Hepatitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Chronic viral hepatitis leads to cirrhosis and HCC, both of which predispose to spontaneous haemoperitoneum.

faqs.question: What is hemoperitoneum?
faqs.answer: Hemoperitoneum is the presence of free blood inside the abdominal cavity, surrounding the organs. It is always caused by internal bleeding from an injured or ruptured structure — such as the liver, spleen, a fallopian tube (in ectopic pregnancy), or a large blood vessel. It is a medical emergency.

faqs.question: What are the main causes of hemoperitoneum?
faqs.answer: The most common cause overall is abdominal trauma injuring the spleen or liver. In women of reproductive age, a ruptured ectopic pregnancy is the leading non-traumatic cause. Other causes include a ruptured aortic aneurysm, a burst liver tumour (such as hepatocellular carcinoma), and bleeding from anticoagulant medications.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of hemoperitoneum?
faqs.answer: Symptoms include sudden severe abdominal or pelvic pain, a rigid tender abdomen, dizziness, rapid heart rate, low blood pressure, and pallor. Shoulder-tip pain from diaphragm irritation by blood (Kehr's sign) may occur. Large bleeds cause fainting or collapse and are life-threatening emergencies.

faqs.question: How is hemoperitoneum diagnosed?
faqs.answer: In trauma patients a bedside FAST ultrasound detects free fluid in the abdomen within minutes. For stable patients, a contrast CT scan precisely locates the bleeding source. A blood pregnancy test must be done in all women of reproductive age. A falling haemoglobin on blood tests supports the diagnosis.

faqs.question: Is hemoperitoneum always an emergency?
faqs.answer: Any confirmed hemoperitoneum should be treated as an emergency until the source is identified and the patient is haemodynamically stable. Even small bleeds can escalate rapidly. A patient who is haemodynamically unstable — low blood pressure, rapid pulse — needs immediate surgical or radiological intervention.

faqs.question: Does hemoperitoneum always require surgery?
faqs.answer: Not always. Haemodynamically stable patients with moderate liver or spleen injuries from blunt trauma are successfully managed without surgery in 60-80% of cases using close observation and, if arterial bleeding is detected on CT, angioembolisation. Unstable patients and ruptured ectopic pregnancies almost always require surgery.

faqs.question: What is FAST ultrasound?
faqs.answer: FAST stands for Focused Assessment with Sonography in Trauma. It is a rapid bedside ultrasound scan taking 2-5 minutes to check four areas of the abdomen and pelvis for free fluid. A positive result — blood seen on screen — in an unstable trauma patient is a direct indication for emergency surgery.

faqs.question: What is Kehr's sign?
faqs.answer: Kehr's sign is pain felt in the left shoulder tip when a patient lies flat and blood irritates the underside of the left diaphragm. It is a classic sign of splenic or left-sided haemorrhage. The pain is referred along the phrenic nerve. It is one of several signs pointing toward intraperitoneal bleeding.

faqs.question: Can hemoperitoneum occur without trauma?
faqs.answer: Yes. Ruptured ectopic pregnancy, a burst liver or spleen tumour, a ruptured aortic aneurysm, and spontaneous bleeding in patients on blood thinners all cause hemoperitoneum without trauma. These non-traumatic cases can be just as rapidly life-threatening.

faqs.question: How much blood is dangerous in hemoperitoneum?
faqs.answer: Even 500 mL of blood in the peritoneum can cause haemodynamic instability in some patients, particularly if bleeding is continuing. Rapid losses of 1,000-1,500 mL typically cause significant shock. The rate of bleeding matters as much as the volume — a slow steady bleed of 1,000 mL may be tolerated where a 500 mL rapid haemorrhage is not.

faqs.question: What is damage-control surgery?
faqs.answer: Damage-control surgery means doing the minimum necessary to stop lethal bleeding and contamination in a critically ill patient, then closing temporarily, correcting coagulopathy and hypothermia in the ICU, and returning to complete definitive repair 24-48 hours later. It is used when the full repair would not be survivable in the immediate emergency.

faqs.question: What is tranexamic acid and how does it help?
faqs.answer: Tranexamic acid is a medication that slows clot breakdown by blocking a process called fibrinolysis. Given within 3 hours of traumatic haemorrhage, it reduces death from bleeding by approximately 9% according to the large CRASH-2 trial. It is now standard in damage-control resuscitation worldwide.

faqs.question: Can hemoperitoneum be caused by blood thinners?
faqs.answer: Yes. Warfarin, heparin, direct oral anticoagulants, and thrombolytics all reduce the blood's ability to clot. Spontaneous intraperitoneal haemorrhage from mesenteric or visceral vessels is a recognised complication, especially at supratherapeutic drug levels or if a minor abdominal injury occurs while anticoagulated.

faqs.question: What happens after non-operative management of a spleen or liver injury?
faqs.answer: The patient is monitored closely in hospital for 24-72 hours with serial blood tests and, if available, repeat imaging. Most are discharged within 3-5 days. Physical activity is restricted for 4-8 weeks to allow healing. Follow-up imaging at 4-8 weeks confirms resolution and guides the return to sport.

faqs.question: How does a ruptured ectopic pregnancy cause hemoperitoneum?
faqs.answer: An ectopic pregnancy, usually in the fallopian tube, grows until it bursts the tube at 6-10 weeks. Blood and gestational tissue pour rapidly into the pelvis and abdomen. The amount of bleeding can be life-threatening within minutes. It is the leading cause of first-trimester maternal death.

faqs.question: Will a ruptured ectopic pregnancy affect my future fertility?
faqs.answer: Having one tube removed (salpingectomy) leaves the other tube intact. Approximately 55-65% of women go on to have a successful intrauterine pregnancy. The risk of ectopic in the remaining tube is around 10-15%. Fertility can also be preserved by salpingostomy in selected cases.

faqs.question: What is the recovery time after hemoperitoneum surgery?
faqs.answer: After laparoscopic salpingectomy for ectopic pregnancy, most women are discharged within 2-3 days and recover fully in 2-4 weeks. After open damage-control laparotomy for trauma, hospital stays range from 1-3 weeks with recovery over 6-12 weeks. The underlying injury grade and any complications determine the timeline.

faqs.question: Can hemoperitoneum come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Delayed re-bleeding occurs in approximately 5-10% of solid-organ injuries managed non-operatively, usually within the first 2 weeks. This is why follow-up imaging is mandatory. Once a definitive haemostatic procedure has been performed and the underlying cause addressed, recurrence is uncommon.

faqs.question: Is hemoperitoneum the same as haemoperitoneum?
faqs.answer: Yes. Hemoperitoneum is the American English spelling and haemoperitoneum is the British English spelling; both refer to the same condition — free blood in the peritoneal cavity.

faqs.question: What blood tests confirm hemoperitoneum?
faqs.answer: No single blood test confirms hemoperitoneum — imaging is required. However, a falling haemoglobin or haematocrit on serial blood counts, rising lactate indicating shock, and coagulopathy provide strong supporting evidence and guide resuscitation decisions alongside imaging.

metaDescription: Hemoperitoneum is blood in the abdominal cavity, often from trauma or ectopic pregnancy. Learn symptoms, FAST ultrasound diagnosis and surgery. Doctor-reviewed.
